title: Chapter 15: The Dark Moon Rises
content: The dark moon rose over the Blackwood Forest like a closed eye.

No moonlight. No silver glow. Just darkness — thick and absolute, the kind of darkness that swallowed torches and muffled sounds. The night when wolf powers were weakest. The night Aldric had chosen for his attack.

Sera stood at the eastern gate with the strike team: Kael at her side, Darius at her back, and ten of the pack's best warriors fanned out behind them. They wore dark clothing — no armor, because armor would slow the transformation. No torches, because torches would give away their position.

"The main force is engaging at the western wall," Darius reported, his voice barely a whisper. "Aldric's rogues hit them ten minutes ago. It's chaos — but it's drawing attention exactly where we want it."
"Which means Aldric thinks we're all at the western wall," Sera said. "He won't be expecting a strike team from the east."
"Let's make sure he doesn't."

They moved through the forest in wolf form — silent, swift, fifteen shadows flowing between the trees. Sera could feel Kael's presence beside her like a second heartbeat. The binding ritual had deepened their connection to something almost telepathic — she didn't need words to know what he was thinking. A flicker of intent, a pulse of emotion, and she understood.
*Left flank. Two rogues. Take them quietly.*

She peeled off from the group with two warriors, circling wide around the enemy position. The rogues were stationed at the edge of Aldric's camp — sentries, their empty eyes scanning the darkness for threats they would never see coming.

Sera struck first. Her silver wolf form was nearly invisible in the moonless night — a ghost, a whisper, a flicker of starlight. Her jaws closed around the first rogue's throat before it could make a sound. Ash and shadow. Nothing left.
The second rogue died the same way — silent and sudden at the hands of her warriors.
*Clear*, she pulsed through the bond. *Moving to the central camp.*
Aldric's camp was a nightmare given form.

Dozens of rogues milled between makeshift tents — not the coordinated army Bren had described, but something worse. A *hive*. The rogues moved with the synchronized precision of insects, their empty eyes tracking the same invisible signals, their bodies responding to a single will.
And at the center of the hive, standing before a bonfire that blazed with sickly green flames, was Aldric.

Kael's uncle was exactly what Sera had expected and nothing like it. He had the Thornwood features — dark hair, sharp cheekbones, the build of an Alpha — but his eyes were wrong. Not golden like Kael's. Not even empty like the rogues'. They were *green*. The same green as the bonfire. The same green as the magic that pulsed through the camp like a heartbeat.
*That's the Shadow Binding*, Sera realized. *He's not just controlling the rogues — he's become part of the magic. It's consuming him.*
*Can you hear him?* Kael's thought was sharp with tension.

Sera reached out with her mind — not toward Kael, but toward Aldric. The link was designed for Kael, attuned to his particular frequency. But the binding ritual had expanded her abilities. Made her more sensitive.
At first, there was nothing. Just the green pulse of the dark magic, drowning out everything else.
Then —

*— so close now, so close, three years of waiting, three years of planning, and tonight the pack will finally be MINE, my nephew's precious wolf-less whore thinks she can stop me but she's nothing, NOTHING, the Silver Queen is a myth, a fairy tale, and when I kill her in front of the pack they'll see —*

Sera recoiled. Aldric's mind was... wrong. Twisted. The curse had eaten away whatever humanity he'd once possessed, leaving only hunger and rage and an obsessive hatred for the nephew who had exiled him.
*He knows about me*, she pulsed to Kael. *He knows I'm here. He thinks I'm a myth — he doesn't believe the prophecy — but he knows I'm coming.*
*Then let's not disappoint him.*
The attack was swift and brutal.

Kael hit Aldric's personal guard first — six rogues, bigger than the others, their eyes burning with the same green fire as the bonfire. The black Alpha wolf tore through them like a storm, his jaws and claws moving with the precision of a decade of combat experience. Fenris was with him now — not as a separate voice, but as an integrated part of his fighting style. The cold Alpha's strategy and the inner wolf's ferocity, combined into something unstoppable.

Sera fought beside him. The silver wolf and the black wolf, moving in perfect synchronization — not because they were communicating, but because they didn't need to. The bond told them everything. Kael feinted left; Sera struck right. Sera drew the rogues' attention; Kael hit them from behind. They were two bodies sharing one mind, one purpose, one war.
Darius and the warriors engaged the outer ring of rogues, keeping the main force occupied while Kael and Sera pushed toward the center.
Aldric watched them come.

He was still standing before the green bonfire, his burning eyes tracking their approach with something that might have been amusement. He didn't look afraid. He didn't look concerned. He looked like a man who was exactly where he wanted to be.
"Nephew." Aldric's voice carried through the chaos, smooth and cold. "You've brought the little Moon-Marked whore to meet me. How thoughtful."
Kael's snarl shook the ground. *"Don't let him bait you. He wants you angry. Angry wolves make mistakes."*

"I don't make mistakes." Kael's voice was ice — but Sera could feel the fury burning underneath. "You murdered thirty of my wolves. You poisoned me. You sent rogues into my castle. And now you're going to die."

"Am I?" Aldric spread his arms. The green fire behind him flared, and the rogues throughout the camp responded — their movements becoming faster, more aggressive, synchronized with their master's will. "I've spent three years building this army. Three years mastering the Shadow Binding. Three years dreaming of the moment I would watch you fall." His burning eyes shifted to Sera. "And you've brought me a bonus. The Moon-Marked. The Silver Queen of the children's stories. Do you know what happens to Moon-Marked wolves when they're exposed to dark magic, girl?"
Sera didn't answer. She was already moving — silver and moonlight and fury, her jaws aimed at Aldric's throat.
He caught her.

Not with his hands — with the magic. The green fire lashed out like a whip, wrapping around her wolf form and yanking her to a halt mid-lunge. She hung in the air, suspended by bands of sickly green light, her mercury eyes blazing with defiance.

"Moon-Marked wolves," Aldric said conversationally, "are uniquely vulnerable to Shadow Binding. Did Magnus not tell you that? Their connection to the Goddess makes them resistant to most dark magic — but *this* magic? This magic was designed specifically to destroy your kind."
The bands tightened. Sera felt her wolf form waver — not transforming back, but *fading*. Like the magic was trying to unmake her. To erase her from existence.
"SERA!"
Kael's roar was pure Alpha fury. He launched himself at Aldric — and the green fire caught him too, wrapping around him, dragging him to a halt beside Sera.

"Two birds with one curse." Aldric smiled. "The prophecy spoke of the Silver Queen and the Alpha of Two Hearts, united against the shadow. But prophecies are not guarantees. They are *warnings*. And I have spent three years learning how to make them wrong."
The magic pulsed. Tightened. Sera felt her bones creak.
And then — through the pain, through the green fire, through the overwhelming wrongness of Aldric's magic — she felt something else.
*Kael. His heartbeat. His fury. His love.*
*"I'm here. I'm right here. I'm not letting go. Whatever happens — I'm not letting go."*
The bond between them flared.

Not the green fire of Aldric's magic — something else. Something silver and gold, wrapping around both of them like a second skin. The binding ritual. The ritual that had permanently connected their wolves. Aldric had known about the prophecy, but he hadn't known about the ritual. He hadn't known that their bond was now unbreakable.
And he hadn't known what happened when you tried to break an unbreakable bond.
The silver-gold light *exploded*.
The green fire shattered. The bands holding Sera dissolved. She hit the ground on four paws, her wolf form solidifying, her mercury eyes blazing with a light that matched the missing moon.
Aldric stumbled backward — the first time she'd seen him lose his composure. "What — that's impossible. The Shadow Binding — "

"Isn't strong enough," Sera growled, her voice rough through wolf jaws. "You built your magic to destroy the Moon-Marked. But I'm not just Moon-Marked anymore. I'm bound to him. And he's bound to me. And together, we're stronger than anything you can throw at us."
She lunged.

This time, Aldric's magic couldn't stop her. The silver-gold light of the bond surrounded her like armor, deflecting the green fire, burning through the dark magic like sunlight through fog. Her jaws closed on Aldric's arm — not killing, not yet, but *holding*. Pinning him in place.
Kael was beside her in an instant. The black wolf's jaws closed on Aldric's other arm, and together they dragged the traitor Alpha to the ground.
"You've lost," Kael said, his voice cold and steady. "Your magic is broken. Your rogues are falling. Your army is being routed as we speak."
"And you," Sera added, "are going to face the pack's justice. Not a quick death in battle. A trial. A public judgment. The same thing you tried to deny me."
Aldric's burning green eyes met hers. For a moment — just a moment — she saw something human flicker in their depths. Something that might have been fear.
"The prophecy," he whispered. "It was real."
"Always was."
The battle ended within the hour.

Without Aldric's control, the rogues reverted to their natural state — chaotic, mindless, easy prey for the pack's warriors. Darius led the clean-up operation, hunting down the remaining creatures while the strike team escorted their prisoner back to the castle.

Sera and Kael walked side by side through the forest — human again, their transformations reversed. Kael's arm was around her shoulders. Her head rested against his chest. They were both covered in blood and ash and the residue of dark magic, and neither of them cared.
"We did it," Sera said. "We actually did it."

"We did." Kael's voice was rough with exhaustion and something that might have been wonder. "The pack is safe. Aldric is defeated. The curse is broken." He stopped walking, turning to face her. "I couldn't have done any of it without you."
"You would have found a way."

"Maybe. But it wouldn't have been this way. It wouldn't have been... whole." He cupped her face in his hands. "You healed me, Sera. Not just the curse — *me*. All the broken pieces I'd been carrying around for years. You showed me that being strong didn't mean being alone. That letting someone in wasn't weakness — it was the bravest thing I could do."
"Fenris helped."

"Fenris *is* me. That's what you helped me understand." His lips curved. "He's still ridiculous. He's still dramatic. He's still composing terrible poetry in the back of my mind right now. But he's not separate anymore. He's just... another part of who I am."

*"I resent the 'terrible poetry' comment. My poetry is EXCELLENT. It's just... niche. Very niche. Specifically niche for one silver-haired wolf who smells like moonlight and just saved the entire pack. That poem is going to be a MASTERPIECE."*
Sera laughed — bright and surprised and full of more joy than she'd ever felt in her life. "He's composing poetry about you. The battle poem. He says it's going to be a masterpiece."
"It's terrible," Kael said.
"It's definitely terrible. But I want to hear it anyway."

He kissed her — there in the moonless forest, surrounded by the ash of their enemies and the quiet sounds of a pack returning home — and for the first time since the trial, since the curse, since the moment she'd heard his inner voice screaming about chocolate and chew toys, Sera let herself believe.
The prophecy was real. The shadow was defeated. And she was exactly where she was supposed to be.
In the arms of the Wolf King, whose heart she could hear — and whose heart she would protect for the rest of her life.
